misc.title: It’s true that the “神の騎士団 God’s Knights” are, in essence, a group that serves Imu—the one at the top of the Celestial Dragons, who are called “gods.” Very few people in the world of One Piece are actu
misc.text: It’s true that the “神の騎士団 God’s Knights” are, in essence, a group that serves Imu—the one at the top of the Celestial Dragons, who are called “gods.” Very few people in the world of One Piece are actually aware of the God’s Knights, but when they hear the term, they would probably understand it not as “Imu’s knights,” but as “the knights of the Celestial Dragons.” That’s because, in the public mind, the word “神 god” refers to the Celestial Dragons as a whole. Vivi: “Reason doesn’t work on them.” Lucci: “A "神 god" doesn’t need to be reasonable.” (Here, "神 god"＝ one of the Celestial Dragons, Charlos) But here, it can also be interpreted as “god ＝Celestial Dragons” in the sense of a group, or “god＝Celestial Dragons” as a concept. What makes this more complicated is that Japanese doesn’t have strict plural rules. There is a plural form like 神々 kamigami (“gods”), but the single word 神 kami can already cover the concept of a god, an individual god, or even a group of gods in One Piece. In other words, Japanese is extremely dependent on context. And in this world to begin with, “god” exists in all kinds of different contexts—and in the end, the Celestial Dragons are also just self-proclaimed gods. And I can understand why some people feel that “Holy Knights” sounds cleaner. But even from the perspective of a native Japanese speaker, I think the translation that captures the nuance of the original most accurately is not “Holy Knights,” but “God’s Knights.” 𝐏𝐚𝐧 ☾ (@garlings_duck) "Knights of God" or "God's Knights" lwk pisses me off so bad bc doesn’t that imply the existence of a singular deity they serve, which I thought is supposed to be … a SECRET? ( holy knights is the superior title ) — https://nitter.net/garlings_duck/status/2055622392110571707#m
